title: Human mathematicians are being outcounterexampled
description: It’s been an interesting few weeks for counterexamples. This post is basically my perspective of what has been going on in the world of formalization, AI tools and, in particular, counterexam…
fullText:
It’s been an interesting few weeks for counterexamples. This post is basically my perspective of what has been going on in the world of formalization, AI tools and, in particular, counterexamples.

Two months ago today (20th May 2026), ChatGPT disproved Erdős’ Unit Distance conjecture in discrete geometry. This is now old news but I had to start somewhere. The announcement was accompanied with testimonies by human mathematicians, many of whom I knew and a few of whom I trusted, saying that they believed the argument (they had been given early access to it and had checked it). The basic structure of the proof is that a profound theorem in number theory due to Golod and Shafarevich from the 1960s could be used to construct a counterexample to the conjecture.

It is now 9 years since I had a mid-life crisis, realised I no longer trusted many human mathematicians when it comes to technical details, discovered Lean, and started to argue that interactive theorem provers should play an important role in the future of mathematics. So of course my first question was “is the counterexample formalized in Lean”. The answer was “no”.

But under a week later (26th May 2026), I got an email from Fields Medallist Mike Freedman. Mike is now the Chief Science Officer for Logical Intelligence, a company cofounded by Turing Award winner and “godfather of AI” Yan LeCun. Mike informed me that their system had autoformalized the entire ChatGPT-generated paper in Lean and could I take a look. I looked, and my post-doc Thomas Browning looked too. And indeed this was what Logical Intelligence had done: they had formalized precisely the statement that the profound theorem of number theory implied the Erdős counterexample. Breakthrough LLM-generated mathematics being formalized in real time. Interesting data point.

Of course there is an elephant in the room here though, the profound theorem of number theory which takes 100+ pages to prove (it needs huge chunks of global class field theory, a theory developed at the beginning of the 20th century and for which there are still no short proofs; it is proving difficult to compress). In 2025 I had run a Clay Summer School with Richard Hill on the formalization of class field theory, and one year later we have nearly done the local case (it is the current PhD project of my student Edison Xie); the global case remained open, and indeed in 2025 formalizing global class field theory seemed like a fantasy.

One month later, on June 26th 2026, my perception of what was possible again changed. Boris Alexeev announced on the Lean Zulip that he had steered ChatGPT to a complete formalization of the Erdős counterexample, assuming nothing beyond the axioms of mathematics. Boris works at OpenAI and had used their new model Sol to do the autoformalization. Boris made the code public and it did not take long for me to realise that somewhere within all this AI-generated (and sometimes horrible, although sometimes decent) code was indeed a proof of some really hard theorems in global class field theory. Also of interest to me was that Sol had generated 1.2 million lines of Lean code in the three weeks that it had worked on the project. Lean’s fantastic (declaration of conflict of interest: I am a maintainer) mathematics library mathlib is only 2.3 million lines of code, and took nine years to write. Perhaps it was at this point that the penny really dropped for me — large AI-generated developments of mathematics are inevitable. One cannot trust AI-generated code so I ran it in a sandbox on my machine (malicious Lean code can run arbitrary commands on your computer — Lean is a programming language, after all). Indeed, it was proving nontrivial theorems about the cohomology of number fields. Wow.

A week after Boris’ revelation, in early July, I was thinking hard about how to run my Formalizing Fermat workshop. This workshop was sponsored by Logos Research, who, like Logical Intelligence (and Harmonic and Axiom AI and Moonshot AI and…) have a tool which can autoformalize mathematics — translating it from human language into Lean — building on mathlib. Logos told me that they were only going to allow 5 people at a time to use their system during the workshop, and there were 25 attendees, so I told all attendees that I would buy them a Claude Max subscription for a month, so they had something to experiment with when it wasn’t their turn for Logos’ tool. The workshop was 6th to 10th July, and the Claude Max subscription would give attendees access to Claude Fable, at least until Tuesday 7th, when it was being switched off. When OpenAI got wind of what I was doing, they also offered all attendees free ChatGPT Pro access for a month; this was a big deal because ChatGPT Sol was coming out on the 9th. So basically all attendees would have access to Sol and Fable for 4 out of the 5 days of the workshop, and Logos’ tool for the entire week. In fact Fable access was not removed on the 7th so we were in even better shape.

I was not sure how good Logos’ tool was going to be, but I wanted a development of the theory of finite flat group schemes in Lean for my ongoing proof of Fermat’s Last Theorem, so I put uploaded some classic papers in the area to Fable and ChatGPT, and got them together to write down an exposition of the theory in natural language. I passed this pdf document over to Logos the day before the workshop, and on the first day of the workshop they said that one of the claims in the pdf was false and they had found an explicit counterexample. Another counterexample! I took a look and indeed the LLM-generated pdf was simply wrong at some point when describing a standard construction; false alarm. I had missed this myself though when reading through the pdf. Interesting how AI had again found a counterexample. I fixed the pdf. I thought it was interesting that the AI didn’t just say “I don’t quite follow this argument”, it instead said “here is a proof that this argument is simply wrong”, a much more powerful statement.

With the development of the theory of finite flat group schemes back on track, I could relax back into the FLT workshop. On Tuesday 7th July I sat opposite Akhil Mathew at lunch; Akhil is a professor of mathematics at UChicago and he was an attendee who had been experimenting with the tools available. We talked about potential questions which AI could work on, and Akhil raised the old question of Grothendieck about whether every finite free group scheme of order n was killed by n. Deligne had proved the result in the commutative case, and Grothendieck had proved it when the base was reduced; Rene Schoof had proved it in more cases, and there had even been a paper by Emiliano Torti published last year, proving it in even more generality. I said that I thought that this was a fabulous thing to get AI thinking about.

The day after the workshop finished, on Saturday 11th July, I got a DM from Akhil telling me that Sol had found a counterexample. He sent me a 12 page pdf. I immediately replied saying that I was not reading AI-generated informal mathematics and could he please formalize the entire thing in Lean. Four hours later he replied again, saying that Fable had autoformalized the entire thing. I scanned over the 1076-line Lean file, checking that the code did not delete all the files on my hard drive (Lean is a programming language, so it can do this). Convinced that it was only theorems, I then compiled it on my laptop and it took me under 5 minutes in total to check that (a) the statement of the claimed theorem used only concepts in mathlib (and thus things like HopfAlgebra can be trusted to mean what mathematicians think of as Hopf algebras) (b) the statement of the claimed theorem was that there was a counterexample and (c) the proof compiled. At this point I knew that we had a counterexample — a group scheme of order 4 which was not killed by 4. I suggested to Akhil that he make a PR to mathlib with the counterexample — which he did. I would have also suggested to him that he draft a press release saying that a machine had solved a 60-year-old question of Grothendieck in algebraic geometry, but somehow by this point I was almost becoming immune to all of this. It wasn’t clear to me that the media would even be able to distinguish between “machine resolves question due to Erdős” and “machine resolves question due to Grothendieck” even though I personally found the latter far more interesting. Of course the Grothendieck counterexample was far far easier than the Erdős one (a thousand lines, not a million), all I’m saying is that it’s an area of mathematics that I personally find more interesting. I pointed out to Akhil that machines seemed to be getting very good at finding counterexamples and suggested that he try the Hodge conjecture next.

I think it’s worth stepping back at this point and surveying what the attitudes of human experts to these sorts of things are. On Tuesday (14th July) I went to work at Imperial and the Grothendieck counterexample was the talk of lunch. A member of the faculty (who I won’t name) said to me that the fact that the counterexample was so easy to find just indicated that humans had not spent enough time thinking about the problem, implying that a 60-year-old question of Grothendieck was not actually that interesting to work on. I didn’t tell him that at some point earlier in my career I had spent a week working hard on the problem. In my mind my colleague is just going through the five stages of grief; right now they seem to be in the denial phase.

After lunch I met with my PhD student Andrew Yang, who had been working on formalizing a modularity lifting theorem in Lean, something which is crucial to my FLT work. Andrew had come to the Logos FLT workshop and now had access to both Sol and Fable. He told me that using these tools he had written 250K lines of Lean code which basically completely finished the project in what was I guess a 2 week period.

A few days earlier I had got an email from a professor in the maths department here at Imperial, expressing surprise that some of our graduate students were paying $200 per month to access models such as Sol and Fable. He said that he thought that these people were crazy. I did not immediately respond. But after meeting with Andrew I emailed the professor back and told him that in my opinion, any PhD student who was not paying $200 per month to access these tools was crazy. In fact during the workshop I learnt from Harvard PhD student Bryan Wang that Harvard were already giving free Fable access to all PhD students, post-docs and faculty at Harvard.

But back to Akhil. I am not sure if he took my idea to disprove the Hodge conjecture seriously. But it looks like he had deeply understood that, with these extraordinary new AI tools, counterexamples might be low-hanging fruit right now. He had discussed with Levent Alpöge the idea of finding more counterexamples in algebraic geometry, and 12 hours ago Levent posted on X that Fable had found a counterexample to the Jacobian Conjecture. This is a big deal — this is a famous question in algebraic geometry which had been open for 100 years and which many people had thought about. It was apparently solved during the 2026 World Cup Final.

I woke up today to a DM from Akhil saying “shall I make another PR?” but this time he was too late — Paul Lezeau had already formalized the counterexample manually and had made a PR to DeepMind’s Formal Conjectures repo. Mathlib does not contain a large list of conjectures in mathematics, but DeepMind’s repo does. The importance of formalization of conjectures by humans is that if humans are agreed that a Lean statement does faithfully capture the idea behind a conjecture, then checking that (possibly AI-generated) Lean code does comprise a proof or disproof of the conjecture is a triviality. Congratulations to Levent, thanks to Akhil for suggesting the problem to him, and thanks to DeepMind for already having formalized the statement and thus making formal verification of the counterexample a triviality.

The Jacobian conjecture is resolved! Wow! The next step in that work is for humans to understand exactly what is going on with the example. For the true value of work like this is to give humans better understanding of mathematics. Indeed Akhil has been working on trying to understand the Grothendieck counterexample in a way which is far deeper than “here is a random presentation of a random ring and a random calculation which shows that something doesn’t work”. What we need next is the insight which can be drawn from these extraordinary examples.

title: Digitally Entertained with Zero Money
description: Considering how much anything costs right now, I realized I can afford nothing. Well, what's a girl to do? Use older tech and forget about the modern world is the answer, apparently.
fullText:
Last edited 1 hour, 36 minutes ago.

Considering how much anything costs right now (bc of AI, blockchain, mining, or whatever the fuck is going on in tech), I have come to a point in life where I realized I can afford nothing. Add to that the fact that my main computer died and I'm struggling financially... well, what's a girl to do?

Use older tech and forget about the modern world is the answer, apparently.

My old ass laptop can barely handle multiple tabs open on a browser, let alone a modern game.1 So I've decided to play what I haven't played in years: Pandemonium! and The Dig. Old games that you can get for super cheap/free and that won't crash my dying laptop.

Coincidentally, a dear friend also gifted me her old 3DS XL and that's what I've been rocking to game off the computer (I must confess I've been struggling to sit in front of a screen to game, I'd rather lie down and slowly nod off to sleep like the old woman that I'm becoming). I modded the 3DS and now I got a bunch of NDS games in there to play as well as the 3DS ones. Animal Crossing New Leaf, the old Tomodatchi Life, Zelda Ocarina of Time and a bunch of old stuff that I've never played in my life.2 Basically, stuff that have contemporary counterparts I'd love to play but absolutely cannot afford. lmao

(It's also super fucking funny that my main computer died AND the screen on my Nintendo Switch got all buggy and I can't play it handheld any longer, bc of course it has to happen all the once amirite)

For music, that's easier to find for free. Other than scavenging Bandcamp for free stuff, we got back to good ol' downloading directly from YouTube, track by track. Like a maniac. (And sometimes torrenting a whole album/discography.) I still got an Apple Music account bc it's on a family plan, but I'll listen to stuff there and then look up the album/song for download. Thankfully that's still easy enough to do.

Also, old ass vinyls in thrift shops/second hand online retailers. I'm not super into collecting them right now (it costs money & I don't own a record player lol), but my sister is, and I've been keeping my modest vinyl stash (acquired pre-financial disgrace) at her place, which I visit constantly.3 So whenever I'm over we'll pop something on the player to listen to.

Sister's also in possession of the DVD player. (Well. It's a PS4 that plays CDs and DVDs, but potato potahto.) My last re-watch of The Lord of the Rings was on it, popping disc after disc. It had been years since I'd touched those DVDs. It also included the BTS stuff, which I never watched when I used streaming. So yeah.

Once again, huzzah for old tech.

And torrenting. Torrenting is so back, you guys -- but I think that, whoever you are, if you're reading this you probably already know that. I can only watch contemporary movies bc of torrenting, but I've also been re-downloading my favorite shows. I used to have a huuuuge digital library back in the early 2010s, but I lost it all in-between computer crashes/upgrades. There's stuff that's pretty easy to find again (The X Files seasons), but other stuff I've been struggling to track down (my Brazilian dubbed episodes of Rupert Bear ).

And then there's books. Now that's the one thing that's really painful to me bc contrary to other countries, physical books now cost an arm, a leg and a kidney in Brazil,4 even used ones. Sometimes a used book will cost as much as a brand new one, which means it's really, really off the table for me most of the time. (Also no such thing as a good public library near me.)

And we're back to downloading ebooks. I feel kinda sad on this one tbh. Even though I've been reading far more digitally in the past few years, I love purchasing physical copies of my favorite ones. I got both an English and a Brazilian-Portuguese copy of Annihilation, for instance. One of my dreams is one day to have all of the Discworld novels on a shelf.

So yeah, books have been a bit of a mixed bag for me emotionally. Earlier this year I purchased a second-hand Boox Palma 1. I think it was my last purchase of anything non-essential, tbh. Or hobby-related. I'm using it almost every day, either to read books, fanfics, listen to audiobooks or music, or (drum-roll) to write.

Torrent stuff. Go for old versions instead of the new ones. Forget they exist even, so you won't feel FOMO. Instead, seek POMO, Pleasure Of Missing Out.

Wanna play Pokémon Mystery Dungeon: Rescue Team DX but absolutely cannot afford it? Download a previous one instead on your old console. Wanna read the latest trendy weird girl book? Use Z-Library/Anna's Archive. (Also jailbreak your Kindle!)

I'm using Archive.org a lot lately, which means I'm also gonna figure out a way to donate a little to them this year, even if that means selling a couple of books to get the money.

If you live in a place that has good public libraries, use them. I've only heard good things about stuff like Libby and Overdrive. Unfortunately, that's not my reality.

Pretend the Steam Machine isn't even real. You don't need a new computer. The modern world isn't real. It certainly doesn't want us in it. Now excuse me, I got a 200k fanfic to read that cost me $0 and will help me forget this world exists for a few hours.

It does run a few indie games that require but a potato to work, but even amongst the indie titles my laptop struggles.↩

Because until the Nintendo Switch I'd never owned a gaming console and also wasn't a big gamer growing up to worry about emulators.↩

She lives with one of my cats who absolutely did not adapt to my new place with my partner's four cats and also I miss her cats (and her, I guess).↩

Because of a mixed bag of reasons, but a lot due to Suzano steadily hiking up prices since 2020. As for the second-hand books, they were always on the more expensive side in Brazil. Idk what to tell you. It only got worse.↩

title: Date set for Smith's first world title defence
description: Dalton Smith will defend his WBC light-welterweight for the first time on 24 October when he takes on mandatory challenger Alberto Puello.
fullText:
Dalton Smith will defend his WBC light-welterweight belt for the first time on 24 October when he takes on mandatory challenger Alberto Puello.

The 29-year-old British fighter will be roared on by the crowd in his home city of Sheffield for his 20th professional bout as he aims to extend his undefeated record.

Smith became world champion for the first time in January thanks to a stunning stoppage of Subriel Matias in New York.

His bout with Dominican former champion Puello was originally scheduled for June but had to be postponed when Smith suffered an injury in training.

"I'm delighted the fight is finally back on and that we'll be defending my WBC world title on October 24 in Sheffield," said Dalton.

"It was obviously frustrating to pick up the injury when I was in such a good place, especially after all the hard work that had gone into camp.

"But injuries are part of boxing. The important thing is that I've taken the time to recover properly, listened to the medical advice and now I'm back training, feeling strong and ready to go again."

Meanwhile, Britain's Ben Whittaker will aim to secure himself a shot at the light-heavyweight title when he takes on Australia's Conor Wallace.

After an impressive debut in the US in June, 29-year-old Whittaker will be back on home soil in an IBF eliminator in Birmingham on 3 October.

Promoters Matchroom Boxing also announced that top prospect Pat Brown will fight English champion John Hedges for the British and Commonwealth cruiserweight titles in Manchester on 19 September.

Brown, 26, is considered one of the most exciting talents in the country and has a perfect record through six professional fights, winning all six by knockout.

title: Uefa president boycotts World Cup final in protest against Fifa
description: Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin did not attend the World Cup final between Argentina and Spain in protest against Fifa.
fullText:
Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin did not attend the World Cup final between Argentina and Spain in protest against Fifa.

While the Slovenian did travel to Mexico City for the opening game between the co-hosts and South Africa, he snubbed the trip to New Jersey on Sunday after a series of decisions taken by world football's governing body.

Uefa reacted strongly after United States striker Folarin Balogun escaped a suspension following an intervention from US President Donald Trump.

This was a key factor in Ceferin's decision not to go to the final.

Uefa had criticised Fifa's decision at the time, calling it "unprecedented, incomprehensible and unjustifiable".

"When the certainty of rules is no longer guaranteed by its guardians, the integrity of the game is at stake and the credibility of a competition is undermined," Uefa said in a statement.

Ceferin is also firmly against many other changes driven by Fifa president Gianni Infantino, including half-time shows. They will not be introduced to the Champions League or the European Championship.

Pop giants Madonna, BTS, Shakira and Justin Bieber were among the acts to perform in the event, which extended the usual 15-minute half-time break to 27 minutes 22 seconds.

Uefa is also strongly against a 64-team World Cup, which Infantino appeared to open the door to during the tournament.

After referee Omar Artan was denied entry to the US before the tournament started, Uefa invited the Somali to officiate the Super Cup between Paris St-Germain and Aston Villa on 12 August.

The high ticket prices have also proved controversial within Uefa.

Uefa will broadly freeze ticket prices for Euro 2028 - meaning supporters will be able to buy five for the price of a single parking space at a World Cup stadium in the United States this summer.

BBC Sport reported on Friday how Uefa has instructed its VARs not to consider diving under the new mistaken identity protocol, as Fifa has during the World Cup. This led to Switzerland striker Breel Embolo being sent off in the quarter-final defeat to Argentina.

Players who cover their mouths in confrontations with opponents will not be shown red cards in Uefa competitions either.

The issue of players covering their mouths became high profile in February when Benfica winger Gianluca Prestianni raised his shirt while speaking to Real Madrid's Vinicius Jr during a Champions League game.

The Argentina international was accused of racist abuse and provisionally banned for one match. Following a Uefa investigation, Prestianni was found guilty of homophobic conduct and banned for six matches - three of which were suspended.

The law change for a red card was the brainchild of Fifa president Infantino, who wanted something which would have "a deterrent effect" at the World Cup.

Paraguay's Miguel Almiron was the first player to be sent off in his side's World Cup group game against Turkey. Arsenal defender Piero Hincapie then saw red in Ecuador's last-16 exit to co-hosts Mexico.

title: What did England learn during ODI series win over India?
description: After beating India 2-1, BBC Sport asks if England are finally building momentum one year out from the World Cup.
fullText:
What a difference three weeks makes.

At the end of June, a dark cloud hung over the England camp, with Test captain Ben Stokes retiring after a 2-1 series defeat by New Zealand.

That campaign brought an end to coach Brendon McCullum's time in charge of the red-ball side, and plenty of questions remain about the future of the Test team, but the New Zealander has enjoyed an upturn in fortunes in July, with his limited-overs outfit beating India 4-0 in a T20 series and 2-1 in one-day internationals.

Defeating the world number one side in both formats, and overtaking them at the top of the rankings in the 20-over game, is no mean feat and England may feel they are now beginning to show the sort of momentum they last displayed in the build-up to their World Cup victory in 2019.

With the next edition of the tournament a little over a year away, BBC Sport takes a look at whether England's 50-over team is finally tracking in the right direction.

England's only significant change in the India series was a new opening combination of Ben Duckett and Jacob Bethell, and it bore fruit at Lord's.

The left-handed duo became England's sixth opening pair since the 2023 World Cup, with Phil Salt, Jamie Smith and Zak Crawley currently out of contention and Will Jacks and Rehan Ahmed considerations for the middle order after spells at the top.

They had a decent start in the first ODI at Edgbaston, putting on 63, but Duckett was dismissed first ball in the second fixture at Cardiff, with Bethell following shortly afterwards as England found themselves 8-2.

In London, however, the pair flogged a tired India attack, taking advantage of benign batting conditions to put on the highest ODI opening stand at Lord's, with Duckett pressing on for his fourth century in the format.

Bethell, who has not previously batted higher than four for England in white-ball cricket, started his ODI opening career with innings of 14 and four, but cut a composed figure at the Home of Cricket.

He played in a typically positive fashion, striking at 97.85 as he plundered 11 fours and two sixes before falling for 91, just short of a second ODI ton.

The 22-year-old's promotion up the order means England can carry an extra bowler, with the hosts fielding three seamers and spinner Adil Rashid alongside all-rounders Sam Curran and Will Jacks in the latter two matches of this series.

If England are to regain the World Cup, having surrendered it meekly in 2023, much of the responsibility will rest on Joe Root's shoulders.

The 35-year-old struggled by his standards in the one-day format for a number of years following the 2019 victory, averaging just 29 between 2020 and 2023, and his record saw him left out of the ODI set-up for the entirety of 2024.

However since his recall, Root has enjoyed a remarkable uptick in form, averaging 81.5 from 21 innings with four centuries.

He has now made six consecutive scores over 50, stretching back to the start of the series in Sri Lanka in January, becoming the first England batter to achieve that feat.

Root's unbeaten 76 at Edgbaston kept England in contention, with a 121-run partnership with Liam Dawson coming between collapses of 6-107 and 4-30, while an undefeated 99 in Cardiff anchored England's successful chase.

Coming in after the record-breaking opening stand on Sunday, Root enjoyed a rare opportunity to cut loose, with his strike-rate of 154.17 the highest of any ODI score over 50 in his ODI career.

If there is a question to be answered about England's side, it is who will fill the seamer slots in the XI.

With the World Cup to be played on the hard decks of South Africa and Zimbabwe, England will likely persist with three outright fast bowlers with the support of Curran.

Spearhead Jofra Archer is an automatic selection, assuming he remains fit, while Gus Atkinson did his cause no harm in his two outings against India, taking 3-50 in Cardiff and keeping to an economy rate of four at Lord's when no other bowler was under six.

The third slot remains up for grabs, with neither Josh Tongue nor Saqib Mahmood consolidating their place in the side.

Tongue took one wicket in two matches and was expensive at Lord's, conceding 43 runs from five overs, while Mahmood took 2-52 in Cardiff but was rotated out for Tongue for the series finale.

Brydon Carse returned to the squad for this series having recovered from wrist and elbow injuries that have kept him out of international cricket, while the selectors have also favoured the all-round skills of Jamie Overton and may return to him once he has recovered from the thigh injury he sustained in the Indian Premier League.

With a five-match Ashes series preceding the World Cup, England may expect at least one seamer to go down with injury, and will want a strong group of pacers ready to step up to the tournament squad.

There is also slight concern about spinner Adil Rashid, who has had a quiet 2026, taking eight wickets in ODIs and only one in this series.

The 38-year-old's economy rate for the year is consistent with recent efforts, remaining under six, while he can still claim to be England's biggest threat in overs 11-40 - taking 32 of their 72 wickets in that period since the start of last year.

England will return to the one-day format at the tail-end of the summer against Sri Lanka, with matches at Chester-le-Street, Headingley and The Oval between 22-27 September.

Their next taste of international cricket will be in the format as they play three matches in Australia in mid-November, before they sample World Cup conditions with three fixtures in South Africa in mid-January.

There is set to be an ODI series against Pakistan in the 2027 summer before the World Cup starts on 4 October.

title: Thank you for making this a World Cup to remember on the BBC
description: BBC Director of Sport Alex Kay-Jelski's message as BBC Sport delivers a record-breaking World Cup across TV, radio, online and social.
fullText:
As an incredible World Cup comes to an end, everyone here at the BBC wanted to say thank you.

Thank you to the 42.8 million people who tuned in to our live match coverage on television or streamed games on iPlayer, the BBC Sport app and website 237 million times. That's a lot of football.

Whether you were one of the 24 million who watched England v Argentina in the semi-final, the late-night 9.1 million who stayed up until 4am for England v Mexico, the 2.4 million who saw Scotland beat Haiti in the middle of the night, or part of the hardcore crew of half a million who gave Iran v New Zealand a chance at 2am and were rewarded with a four-goal thriller.

Thank you to those of you who trusted us to not spoil your mornings, coming to iPlayer or the BBC Football YouTube channel every day for our eight-minute highlight packages. There were so many of you that we saw the biggest ever day and week in iPlayer's history.

Thank you to the 57.2 million unique browsers who used our app and website 1.7 billion times, watching clips of our games, interviews and journalism, following our amazing live pages and reading stories from our journalists covering everything from the Trump-Fifa-Balogun scandal to tactical trends and the US entry denial of Somalian referee Omar Artan.

World Cup final watched by 15.8m people on BBC

And to those of you who spent ages deep in our new 3D tool, watching the game as if you were Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo or Erling Haaland charging down the goalkeeper - it was used almost five million times. We love trying new things and you using them helps us decide what to do next.

Thank you to the millions of you on the move tuning in to the games on Radio 5 Live or BBC Sounds, realising there are few better ways to sit on the edge of your seat than listening to John Murray, Ian Dennis and the team. And to those who helped the visualised Football Daily podcast reach more people than ever before.

As for those of you whose fingers are tired from six weeks of swiping more than two billion times through our social and digital content, thank you for devouring our behind-the-scenes footage of Harry Kane's hilarious high-pitch interview, our attempts to introduce Olivier Giroud to Irn-Bru, our YouTube tactical dissections and, of course, Wayne Rooney, Joe Hart and Micah Richards' rowing trip down the Hudson.

Thank you to all the new people using the BBC, buying licence fees from QR codes or finding our app, socials or shows for the first time. It's great to have you with us.

There's lots more we have to offer if you stick around too, whether that is downloading our app with its swipeable video feed, following your favourite sport or team for a personalised experience, or checking out our range of documentaries and podcasts across iPlayer and Sounds.

It doesn't have to be sport either - there are amazing dramas, comedies, music, children's shows and news coverage across the BBC, to mention just a few things.

Mostly, however, you spent time with the BBC during the World Cup - and 94% of you in the country did - thank you for choosing us and trusting us.

We loved bringing all the action, fun and drama to you.

title: An excavation in Taiwan searches for remains of Japanese soldiers lost in WWII ship sinking
description: Heads bowed, experts offered their respects on Monday ahead of an excavation aimed at recovering the remains of Japanese soldiers from World War II in southern Taiwan.  The site is linked to the sinki...
fullText:
PINGTUNG, Taiwan (AP) — Heads bowed, experts offered their respects on Monday ahead of an excavation aimed at recovering the remains of Japanese soldiers from World War II in southern Taiwan.

The site is linked to the sinking of a military transport vessel and other ships in the Bashi Channel after they were attacked by U.S. forces in 1944. According to historian Kazuko Tachi, who is also an activist involved in the recovery efforts, more than 4,700 soldiers and crew members died while 65 survived.

Taiwan, a self-governing island claimed by China as its territory, was a Japanese colony from 1895 until the end of the war.

"It is believed that many bodies from the sunken vessel reached this shore," said Tatsuaki Inoue, representing the Japan Association for the Recovery and Repatriation of War Casualties.

The excavation in Hengchun Township is expected to continue for a week. If any remains believed to be Japanese are found and DNA samples can be extracted, they will be sent to Japan during a future mission.

Japan enacted a law in 2016 to accelerate the recovery of war remains, an effort that has been extended through 2030 and includes expanded DNA testing.

Official Japanese records say about 41,900 soldiers who fought for Japan during WWII died in Taiwan. Around 26,300 bodies have been recovered and 15,600 remain missing.

The last excavation in Taiwan took place in 1975, when 242 bodies were found.

"We feel very sorry that we kept them waiting for such a long time," Inoue said. "But on the other hand, we are very happy that we came to be able to excavate in search of their remains."

Historians, residents and Japanese volunteers living in Taiwan spent years conducting interviews and collecting historical records to establish what might have happened to those washed ashore.

They believe the bodies may have been gathered by residents and placed together in a rocky coastal area near the discharge outlet of Taiwan's Third Nuclear Power Plant, where they may still lie.

More than 2.4 million Japanese soldiers died overseas during WWII. The remains of approximately 1 million have yet to be recovered.

In response to public demand, the Japanese government implemented three plans to recover remains between the 1950s and the 1970s, said Akira Nishimura, professor of religious studies at the University of Tokyo.

"For bereaved families who have not received the remains of their loved ones, the lack of certainty regarding where and how their relatives met their end — and the feeling that they were unable to give them a proper sendoff — is likely one of the main reasons why the recovery of remains continues even 80 years later," Nishimura said.

He added that religious organizations and volunteers have played a key role in recovering remains and holding memorial services, offering comfort to bereaved families while helping preserve the memory of those who never returned.

In Pingtung, where Monday's excavation began, two sites honor Japan's war dead.

A monument dedicated to the spirits of Japanese service members who perished stands beside Chaoyin Temple, where an inscription reads: "We pray for the peace of all humanity in the world."

The temple is dedicated to those who died in the Bashi Channel during the war. Inside stands a Japanese flag beside a statue of the Buddhist Bodhisattva Ksitigarbha, who is believed to guide and protect the souls of the departed.

Japan's wartime losses were unusual in that many soldiers and sailors died at sea, leaving their remains unrecovered, said Beatrice Trefalt, associate professor of Japanese studies at Monash University in Australia.

"Because of the enormous amount of uncertainty about who died or not, or how, the search for remains has been a constant in postwar Japanese history," Trefalt said.

She added that many families believe the government has a responsibility to acknowledge those who were conscripted and died during the conflict.

Tachi learned about the fate of the Japanese soldiers who died in the Bashi Channel only after moving to Taiwan. After locals recounted that history, she decided to get involved in recovering their remains.

"Those Taiwanese uncles taught me the importance of holding a memorial service for the war dead," Tachi said. "It's been more than 80 years since the war ended but I believe it is important to pray for the war dead, which I will keep on doing."

Hernández reported from Beijing. AP journalists Mayuko Ono and Mari Yamaguchi in Tokyo contributed.

title: Rodri's stock at all-time high as he adds to trophy collection
description: Rodri cements status as one of the world's best midfielders after World Cup and Golden Ball triumphs.
fullText:
As Spain captain Rodri lifted the World Cup trophy high into the New Jersey sky, he added another trophy to his already glittering record.

A Ballon d'Or, one European title, one Nations League, four Premier League trophies, one Champions League and numerous other domestic triumphs. And now the greatest prize of them all.

The Manchester City playmaker has long been revered for his club exploits, but now his stock is at an all-time high as he added world champion to his list of accolades when Spain beat Argentina 1-0 in the World Cup final on Sunday in New Jersey.

Not only did the Spain captain steer his side to a first triumph since 2010 and stitch a second star on the national shirt, but he was also crowned player of the tournament.

The 30-year-old becomes only the fourth men's player to win the World Cup, Euros, Champions League and Ballon d'Or, joining legends Gerd Muller, Franz Beckenbauer and Zinedine Zidane.

But until recently, this level of success perhaps seemed out of reach for Rodri.

After rupturing his anterior cruciate ligament in 2024, questions were asked about whether he could ever really return to the heights that saw him clinch the Treble with City in 2023 and claim the Euros with Spain a year later.

Such a major injury takes time to recover from and it is only in glimpses during last season that we saw the Rodri of old.

Now it seems the Spaniard was simply biding his time, patiently waiting for a full return to fitness in order to peak at the World Cup in the United States, Canada and Mexico.

Rodri's future in Manchester remains uncertain with only one year left on his contract and Real Madrid reportedly coming knocking.

It begs the question what next for Spain's conductor?

If 19-year-old Lamine Yamal is the flair and creativity of the Spanish team, Rodri is the puppet master.

His role in the middle of the park may often go under the radar, but he is the one stringing it all together.

Throughout Spain's World Cup campaign he completed 756 passes, the most in a single edition on record since 1966.

He featured in all eight of La Roja's matches en route to glory, topping the metrics for touches (910), passes in the opposition half (428), line-breaking passes (122) - including in the final third (76).

There are only three instances on record (since 1966) of a player completing 100+ passes in a World Cup final. Brazil's Dunga in 1994 (130), defender Pau Cubarsi (121) and Rodri (101) - both in this year's edition.

"Rodri was absolutely outstanding," said Spanish football journalist Guillem Balague.

"He is not only the guy who ran the longest in the whole tournament and average per game, the amount of passes he gives to beat defenders, he is at his sharpest, he is at Ballon d'or level. This is Rodri's team."

A second Ballon d'Or does not seem out of reach, although he has perhaps not played enough club football to make him the favourite for the individual accolade in a stacked field.

Rodri is currently being linked with a move to Real Madrid, where returning manager Jose Mourinho is reportedly very keen on the composure, defensive ability and ball control of the former Atletico and Villarreal midfielder.

New City boss Enzo Maresca is unlikely to be interested in letting Rodri go but, with only one year left on his contract, there is naturally a degree of uncertainty about the situation.

One thing is for sure, his performance at this World Cup has placed him as a hot property - one that City would surely not want to part with but would also fetch a premium price if they did.

Returning from an ACL injury is a notoriously difficult task.

Having suffered the injury in September 2024, mere months after Spain's Euros triumph, Rodri missed the rest of that season then featured sporadically last campaign.

He made 33 appearances across all competitions for City but suffered a number of injury setbacks and, at times, looked a shadow of the player he was before the injury.

Former City boss Pep Guardiola predicted in October last year that we would not see the best of Rodri until the World Cup.

And, as has so often been the case, the Catalan was right.

"I've gone through a logical process that every football player has to go through after an injury like this," Rodri said after Spain's triumph.

"It takes a year to get back to your best fitness level and then one more on top of that to feel like yourself again as the player you were.

"You need to be patient, and you need to believe and be confident in yourself."

It was a frank conversation with Guardiola, where Rodri told him he needed to stop to get his body right for the World Cup, that changed the trajectory of his recovery and ensured he was back to his best for the summer tournament.

"To get where I am today in my current form, I had to be clever," Rodri added.

"I had to know when to stop playing and when to carry on when I needed to, and that's part of what you need to do to recover properly.

"It's been a very tough time. I just want the new generations to see my example as an opportunity.

"When you go down, you can rise again. This is my philosophy for my entire life.

"Of course, sometimes things go well, things go wrong, but always the positiveness."

title: Could Argentina face action after post-final trouble?
description: Argentina are likely to face a Fifa investigation following the ugly scenes at the end of the World Cup final.
fullText:
Argentina are likely to face a Fifa investigation following the ugly scenes at the end of the World Cup final.

Fifa had already begun the process of potential disciplinary action against Argentina when their players held a banner in support of their country's claims to the Falkland Islands following the 2-1 semi-final win against England.

After the 1-0 defeat on Sunday, members of the Argentina team and backroom staff were involved in altercations with Spain players as they began to celebrate their World Cup success.

Any player or coach involved in the fracas at the MetLife Stadium could face suspensions, while the Argentine Football Association (AFA) may also be fined.

It was initially reported by Fifa that Argentina's Leandro Paredes had been sent off by referee Slavko Vincic after he pushed Spain's Eric Garcia by the throat.

Paredes' red card was removed from the record shortly afterwards and there is now no evidence of any disciplinary action being taken at the time.

Argentina had already had one player dismissed, with Enzo Fernandez receiving a second yellow card in second-half stoppage time.

Fifa's disciplinary committee will now await the official match reports before taking any action.

As the final whistle blew in New Jersey, a few Argentina players - including Lionel Messi - slumped to the turf.

Others stood there motionless as Spain's players raced across the pitch in jubilation.

Rodri, who had been substituted in extra-time, ran over from the dugout to join his team-mates.

As the Manchester City midfielder went past Nahuel Molina, the Argentina defender appeared to punch Rodri on the arm or stomach.

This was the fuse which ignited the trouble.

Rodri confronted Molina, but it was the Argentina player who thrust forward in an aggressive manner.

Garcia ran over to back up his team-mate at which point Paredes got involved too, jabbing Garcia in the throat.

Unused substitute Gavi tried to intervene, only to be forced to the ground by Thiago Almada.

Paredes then pushed Gavi to the floor by putting his hand in the Spaniard's face.

Argentina assistant coach Roberto Ayala then looked to be holding Garcia around the throat. He then appeared to strike Spain midfielder Dani Olmo.

"Paredes threw two or three haymakers into someone's face. He went for them," Alan Shearer said on BBC One's coverage. "There is no place or space for that.

"We know how much it means to them and we know how much it hurts to lose, but there is a way to lose. Too many times we have seen that from Argentina. The reaction after the final whistle is terrible."

Joe Hart added: "The game's done, they can have absolutely no complaints about what happened today. Disgusting behaviour."

But Argentina boss Lionel Scaloni, who had attempted to calm down the situation, appeared to play down the incidents in his post-match interview.

"We are gracious in victory, and we must be gracious in defeat," he said. "Today, we are showing that we know how to lose.

"We lost the match, and we accept it, but that doesn't mean we stop living or forget everything we did to get here."

Fifa's disciplinary committee has a number of options open to it.

It could look to issue three-match bans for violent conduct, but it is more likely to use its disciplinary code to give stronger charges.

Article 13 covers offensive behaviour and the principles of fair play.

The actions of the players and staff could be judged as "violating the basic rules of decent conduct" and "behaving in a way that brings the sport of football and/or Fifa into disrepute".

The Fifa disciplinary committee takes into account "both aggravating and mitigating circumstances", and it has the power to enforce the ban globally.

Fifa used this clause to charge then-Spanish football federation president Luis Rubiales after kissing Jenni Hermoso on the lips after Spain's Women's World Cup final win against England.

Rubiales was banned from all football-related activities for three years in 2023 and lost an appeal to get it overturned.

Uruguay striker Luis Suarez was suspended from all football-related activity for four months for biting Italy defender Giorgio Chiellini at the 2014 World Cup.

It meant the Liverpool player missed the first nine games of the Premier League season.

It is unlikely that Paredes, Molina and Ayala will face bans as long as this.

The AFA, however, has faced issues with the behaviour of its players in recent years.

Argentina faced charges related to "offensive behaviour and violations of the principles of fair play" and "misconduct of players and officials" following the 2022 World Cup final.

The South Americans beat France on penalties but were criticised for the way they celebrated.

Aston Villa goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez made a lewd gesture with his Goalkeeper of the Tournament trophy, before being filmed in the dressing room mocking France star Kylian Mbappe.

Argentina were fined £18,300 and no action was taken against any player involved.

But Martinez was banned for two games in 2024 for 'offensive behaviour' in World Cup qualifiers against Chile and Colombia.

Martinez repeated his infamous celebration using a replica Copa America trophy following a 3-0 win over Chile. Four days later, he hit a camera with his gloves as a cameraman approached him on the pitch after a 2-1 defeat by Colombia.

At this World Cup, the AFA is already facing a probable fine for the banner held up after the England game.

Their players celebrated while holding a cloth which read "Las Malvinas son Argentinas", which translates as "The Falklands are Argentine".

In 2014, the AFA was fined £20,000 after its players held up a banner with the same message before a friendly against Slovenia.

It is possible that Fifa could issue bans to the players who were holding it: Tottenham's Cristian Romero, former Spurs player Giovani Lo Celso and Manchester United's Lisandro Martínez.

After Spain's 2-1 Euro 2024 final win over England, their captain Alvaro Morata and team-mate Rodri were banned for one game by Uefa after they chanted "Gibraltar is Spanish" during their victory celebrations.

So when could Fifa make a decision on any potential charges?

After the 2022 World Cup final on 18 December, Fifa officially charged Argentina on 13 January but it took another month for a decision to be reached.

There will not be a resolution any time soon.

Next Men's World Cup set for three continents. What to know about FIFA 2030
description: Come the next World Cup, Spain will defend its current champion title on home soil.
fullText:
In the next edition of the FIFA Men's World Cup, Spain will defend its championship on home soil, as the European country will host World Cup 2030 jointly with Portugal and Morocco.

The next edition of the Men's World Cup will take place in the Mediterranean, particularly in Morocco, Portugal and Spain, the FIFA Council confirmed.

The 2030 tournament will mark the 100 anniversary since the first World Cup was contested in Uruguay in 1930. To commemorate a century of history, FIFA agreed to host a centenary celebration in the Southern Cone of South America, specifically in Paraguay, Uruguay and Argentina. All three countries are a few hours drive away from each other.

One game will be played in each of the South American countries. All other matches will take place in the Mediterranean host countries.

"In 2030 the FIFA World Cup will unite three continents and six countries, inviting the entire world to join in the celebration of the beautiful game," FIFA stated.

The centenary ceremony will take place in Uruguay's capital, Montevideo, five hours away from Argentina's capital, Buenos Aires.

FIFA said that the national soccer team of each of the six host countries will automatically qualify for the tournament.

Saudi Arabia will host the 2034 Men's World Cup across five host cities. The country will be the only hosting nation.

Juan Carlos Castillo is a New Jersey-based trending reporter for the USA Today network. He covers weather, and national events focusing on how they affect New Jersey, Pennsylvania and Delaware.

This article originally appeared on Asbury Park Press: Next Men's World Cup set for three continents. What to know about FIFA 2030

title: ‘I’m A Top Contender’ — Luke Lessei Reasserts ONE World Title Credentials After Rebound Win Over Mohamed Younes Rabah
description: "The Chef" stirred the pot about what comes next after his big win this past Friday.
fullText:
Luke “The Chef” Lessei has always been at his most dangerous when the doubters are loudest, and ONE Fight Night 45 was the most emphatic evidence yet.

Hungry to respond after a recent setback, the Iowa native delivered a commanding unanimous decision over “The Eagle” Mohamed Younes Rabah in the featherweight Muay Thai main event, which aired live in U.S. primetime from Bangkok’s iconic Lumpinee Stadium on Friday, July 17.

Lessei’s striking has always been a genuinely captivating blend of surgical precision and explosive flair, but consistency has sometimes been the missing ingredient.

The painful TKO defeat to now-ONE Interim Featherweight Muay Thai World Champion Nico “King of the North” Carrillo last November raised uncomfortable questions about his place among the division’s elite.

Against Rabah, a sharper, more deliberate Lessei stepped into the ring, looking every bit like a fighter reborn. Behind that renewed composure lay the defining trait of a warrior who never buckles when his back is against the wall.

“It felt great. I’m always happy to get a win. Fun fact, I’ve actually never lost two fights in a row. So I got that streak alive coming off a loss against a champion and a win against a top contender like Younes, who’s one of my friends.”

The opening round did not unfold as Lessei had envisioned, with Rabah finding his range quickly and seizing the advantage with clean, punishing combinations.

Sensing the momentum shifting, the 30-year-old tactician absorbed a crucial corner adjustment that changed the course of the fight entirely.

“My coach started telling me to trust my boxing and double up my hands a little bit more. I started to find my range with the jab, and it just brought me more confidence.

“I realized that the long straight shots were going to win me the rest of that fight, so I was just trusting in my actual reach, trusting in my coach.”

Lessei took full control as the fight progressed, his shots landing with greater frequency and force. A thunderous hook in the final stanza rocked Rabah badly, briefly buckling the Algerian’s knees before his toughness kept him upright.

Although all three judges awarded him the nod, Lessei’s exacting nature craves something far more emphatic. The cerebral finisher wanted something more decisive, and he is already thinking about how to close that gap.

“There was one shot in the third round — a big hook, and my coach tells me like it should have been an eight-count. I wish I had gotten that eight-count. I could have smelled blood and went for the kill, chasing the 50K bonus.

“But it is what it is. I’m happy with the shots that I landed. I can always improve. I can always do better, and I think I could push the pace even more.”

Luke Lessei’s win over Mohamed Younes Rabah means far more than a return to form, thrusting him firmly back into featherweight contention.

Looking ahead, three names stand out. A rematch with Nico Carrillo tops the list, while Bampara Kouyate, who handed him a buzzer-beater knockout, remains unforgotten. The American striker also remains deeply intrigued by a potential showdown with Thai standout Shadow Mavinn.

Lessei’s desire to test his mettle against the division’s best burns so deeply that he would gladly do so under kickboxing rules if need be:

“I just heard [Shadow] has a broken jaw right now, so I’m not sure how soon he’ll be back, but all three of the names — Bambi, Nico, Shadow, whoever.

“I’ve always been interested in putting the big gloves back on, doing kickboxing, since there’s no big-glove Muay Thai. But, yeah, I love banging in whatever style it is for striking.”

With reigning ONE Featherweight Muay Thai World Champion Tawanchai PK Saenchai sidelined through injury, the division is ripe with opportunity — and Lessei has every intention of seizing it.

For now, “The Chef” is savoring Friday night’s result. The 26 pounds of gold remain the ultimate destination, and every performance is another step toward it.

“A chance to fight for the belt is always at the top of the list, so I can’t really decide. I’m just down to fight whoever. I just love fighting for ONE. I love performing for my fans.

“I want to make sure I enjoy my win, and I focus on being present, but I think I’m a top contender. I want to keep reminding people with good performances.”

title: Barcelona expecting a public gesture from priority target with World Cup over
description: The FIFA World Cup final was all that was hindering progress in the Barcelona-Julian Alvarez saga, for the player was an integral member of Lionel Scaloni’s side and did not wish to cause any distur...
fullText:
The FIFA World Cup final was all that was hindering progress in the Barcelona-Julian Alvarez saga, for the player was an integral member of Lionel Scaloni’s side and did not wish to cause any disturbance in the dressing room during the tournament.

In recent days, bridges between Alvarez and Atletico Madrid took a big hit, especially after the Spanish club’s CEO openly declared that the player was not for sale at any price.

He openly called out Barcelona and Joan Laporta for their approaches and maintained that the player would not leave, thereby breaking a promise they made to the player last season.

As reported by SPORT now, the moment of truth has now arrived in the Alvarez saga now that the World Cup is complete.

Alvarez must now take a stand. (Photo by Elsa/Getty Images)

Alvarez is furious with Atletico Madrid for going back on their word, for they had promised to enable a departure should the player desire it at the end of the season.

Now that the international tournament is complete, there is an expectation within Barcelona that the Argentine is willing to step forward and make a move from his side.

The ball is now in Alvarez’s court, and while a departure is still complicated, a public announcement of his desire to leave and play for Barcelona can change a lot.

It will put pressure on Diego Simeone’s side and force the negotiating doors open.

If the Spanish outlet is to be believed, Alvarez wishes to clarify a lot of hidden truths about his situation and how he has been wronged. The time has now arrived, and Barcelona are expecting the said gesture from his side.

Barcelona will not wait for Alvarez forever. (Photo by Eric Alonso/Getty Images)

The World Cup eclipsed a major part of the ongoing summer transfer window, and while it is now past us, so is the majority of the negotiating window clubs had.

Barcelona are thus looking to be smart about its situation and do not wish to throw all their eggs in one basket.

The Argentine striker is the dream signing, but the board have reportedly set a deadline of 31st July to decide on his transfer.

Should no gesture arrive and Atletico Madrid continue to keep a firm stance, they will start looking for alternative options.

title: 'The Era of Red, Blue & Gold!': IPL champions RCB shares unique congratulatory message for FIFA World Cup winners Spain
description: With Spain lifting the FIFA World Cup yesterday, current and back-to-back IPL champions, RCB shared a unique congratulating message for the world champions.
fullText:
'The Era of Red, Blue & Gold!': IPL champions RCB shares unique congratulatory message for FIFA World Cup winners Spain originally appeared on Cricket News. Add Cricket News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

RCB congratulated Spain on their FIFA World Cup triumph with a themed post.

Ferran Torres scored in extra time as Spain beat Argentina 1-0 in New Jersey.

Both RCB and Spain have now completed back-to-back major title successes.

Spain were crowned world champions on Sunday night, with substitute Ferran Torres striking in the 106th minute to seal a 1-0 win over Argentina at the New York New Jersey Stadium in East Rutherford.

It was a final Spain dominated throughout, managing 20 shots to Argentina's two and taking the game away from a side reduced to 10 men after Enzo Fernandez was sent off for a second bookable offence in stoppage time.

The tributes arrived from every corner of the world of sport, and among the more unusual came from Indian cricket. Royal Challengers Bengaluru, the back-to-back IPL champions, posted a message linking their own success with Spain's.

The franchise shared an image combining Spain's celebrations with their own title-winning moments from 2025 and 2026, drawing a line between two teams who have each retained their status at the top.

"Congratulations to Spain on winning the FIFA WC. The Era of Red, Blue & Gold!" the RCB account posted on X.

Congratulations to Spain on winning the FIFA WC. The Era of Red, Blue & Gold! ⭐️🏆#PlayBold#ನಮ್ಮRCBpic.twitter.com/1wKxZw0dcR

The colour reference nods to the red and gold that both Spain and RCB carry, while the blue points to the Barcelona core running through this Spain side, with Torres, Lamine Yamal and Pau Cubarsi all products of the Catalan club.

The parallel between the two sides holds up. RCB ended an 18-year wait for a maiden IPL title in 2025 before defending it in 2026, while Spain followed their Euro 2024 triumph with a second World Cup crown, 16 years after their first in 2010.

Franchise accounts posting about other sports usually feel forced, but this one has a genuine thread running through it. Both teams spent years being described as talented underachievers before finally converting that potential into silverware.

The comparison also lands on the method rather than just the results. Spain won this tournament by control, conceding a single goal across the entire competition, while RCB's back-to-back titles were built on Andy Flower turning a batting-heavy side into a balanced one.

There is a commercial logic, too. RCB has the largest social following of any IPL franchise, and a World Cup final watched by hundreds of millions offers an obvious moment to reach beyond cricket, particularly with Indian audiences increasingly following European football.

fullText:
The Ballon d’Or race has taken another dramatic turn following the conclusion of the 2026 World Cup, with Harry Kane now heading the betting after another remarkable campaign for club and country.

Spain’s World Cup triumph has also seen Lamine Yamal and Rodri shorten significantly in the market after starring throughout the tournament, while Lionel Messi’s run to a second consecutive World Cup final keeps the Argentine firmly among the favourites.

Below, we assess the leading contenders in the latest Ballon d’Or betting.

Harry Kane has moved to the head of the market after arguably the finest season of his career.

The England captain finally ended his wait for major silverware by helping Bayern Munich to another Bundesliga title before leading the Three Lions to the World Cup semi-finals. Kane once again finished among Europe’s leading goalscorers, combining relentless finishing with his trademark creativity.

Although England fell short against Argentina, Kane’s consistency across the entire campaign leaves him as the bookmaker’s favourite for football’s biggest individual prize.

Lamine Yamal continues to rewrite the record books.

Still only a teenager, the Barcelona winger inspired Spain to World Cup glory with another series of match-winning performances on the biggest stage. His creativity, dribbling and composure regularly separated him from defenders throughout the tournament, cementing his status as one of the world’s elite attackers.

Winning the World Cup has propelled Yamal into serious Ballon d’Or contention, and few players possess stronger momentum heading into the final months of voting.

Lionel Messi was priced at 50/1 to win the Ballon d’Or when the 2026 World Cup got underway, but his stunning performances for Argentina have dramatically changed the picture.

The 39-year-old has scored eight goals in six matches so far, inspiring the defending champions to another World Cup Final, and shrunk his Ballon d’Or odds to 4/1.

The Argentina captain guided the reigning champions to a second successive World Cup final and once again produced decisive moments throughout the tournament. His creativity remained central to Lionel Scaloni’s side, while his influence on and off the pitch reinforced his standing as one of the game’s greatest players.

Although Argentina narrowly missed out on retaining their title, Messi’s performances ensure he remains firmly in the Ballon d’Or conversation.

Rodri reminded everyone why he remains one of football’s most influential players.

The Manchester City midfielder controlled games throughout Spain’s victorious World Cup campaign and capped another outstanding year by lifting the biggest trophy in international football. His ability to dictate possession, break up attacks and deliver in decisive moments makes him one of the leading candidates.

Few midfielders combine consistency and silverware as effectively as Rodri, making 4/1 a fair reflection of his credentials.

Kylian Mbappe rarely stays out of Ballon d’Or discussions for long.

The France captain enjoyed another prolific season at club level and continued to deliver on the international stage despite Les Bleus falling short of another World Cup final. His pace, finishing and ability to decide the biggest matches keep him among the leading contenders. Mbappe became the all-time World Cup top goalscorer this summer and now has 22 goals to his name.

Another strong domestic campaign means Mbappe cannot be discounted despite drifting slightly in the betting.

Jude Bellingham (20/1) enjoyed another excellent campaign despite England’s semi-final exit, while Ousmane Dembele (20/1) remains an outsider after another impressive season.

Erling Haaland (33/1) will hope another fast start to the season keeps him in contention, while Khvicha Kvaratskhelia (33/1) and Pau Cubarsi (33/1) complete the current list of leading outsiders

title: Spain Humiliates Trump After He Refuses to Get Out of Victory Photos
description: Donald Trump presented Spain’s World Cup team with the trophy ... and then refused to leave.
fullText:
Spain’s World Cup-winning team has made it abundantly clear that they did not want Donald Trump in their victory photos.

The official account for Spain’s national football league shared an altered image of the country’s celebration in New Jersey Sunday, snipping the U.S. president out of photos after he refused to leave the dais. The FIFA World Cup account also cropped Trump out of the shot, leaving only the president’s shoulder in view.

Trump handed the World Cup trophy to Spain’s team alongside FIFA President Gianni Infantino, after they beat Argentina 1-0. But the U.S. president then refused to leave the picture, apparently trying to run out time before the traditional trophy lift by chatting with the players as he jockeyed for a prime position in front of the group.

Spain’s team, however, was not having it. Video footage of the historic celebration captured several players politely pushing Trump away as they prepared for their official photograph.

Spain’s players refused to celebrate with Trump after the U.S. president attempted to insert himself into the middle of their victory photo. He was politely pushed aside. pic.twitter.com/x82yxxCZgw

— Anonymous (@YourAnonCentral) July 20, 2026

Yet Trump stayed anyway, lingering beside the team even after repeated attempts by Infantino to pull him off the stage. He finally exited only after the team lifted its trophy and blew the confetti cannons in a glaring attempt to ensure he’d be included in the photograph.

His insistence on remaining in frame followed weeks of scathing remarks by Trump against Spain and its prime minister, Pedro Sánchez, over NATO defense spending. Trump has referred to Spain as “hopeless,” derided its people as “bad,” and also threatened to “cut off all trade” with the longtime U.S. ally as he ramped up his NATO disputes. In the months prior to that, he also raged against Spain after they refused to let the U.S. military use Spanish bases to bomb Iran.

None of that hostility was on display after the game, however—at least publicly. Back at Joint Base Andrews, Trump said he had spoken to Spain and “congratulated them on having a great team.” He also noted that he had “no tension” with the country’s leadership.

“I have no tension with anybody,” Trump remarked cavalierly.

title: Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin boycotts World Cup final in protest against Fifa
description: Fifa has come under heavy scrutiny during the 2026 World Cup, with the debacle surrounding Folarin Balogun just one aspect of Uefa’s discontent
fullText:
Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin stayed away from the World Cup final in protest, with the Folarin Balogun affair understood to be just one aspect of his organisation’s discontent with Fifa.

The Times was first to report on Monday that the Slovenian had stayed away, with the relationship between European football’s governing body and the game’s global federation under severe strain.

It’s understood Ceferin chose not to attend the match in New Jersey, with the Uefa president’s unhappiness understood to cover a number of aspects of Fifa’s handling of the World Cup.

Uefa was publicly critical of the decision by Fifa’s disciplinary committee to suspend a one-match ban for United States striker Balogun, calling it “unprecedented, incomprehensible and unjustifiable” and saying it had “crossed a red line”.

The suspension of the ban followed a call from US president Donald Trump to Fifa president Gianni Infantino asking for a “review” of Balogun’s red card, raising questions of political influence in Fifa’s decision-making.

That influence certainly did not appear to be reciprocal, with Fifa powerless to prevent the US authorities sending home Somali referee Omar Artan before the tournament had even started. Uefa subsequently appointed Artan to officiate next month’s Uefa Super Cup final.

Fifa also failed to ensure equitable treatment for Iran compared to the other 47 nations, with the Asian nation subject to strict conditions on their entry to and exit from the US. Their coach, Amir Ghalenoei, said his team were the “most oppressed” at the World Cup, while US Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin said he “danced a happy dance” when the team were eliminated.

There is also discontent at Uefa over Fifa’s apparent willingness to bend or break football’s laws at the tournament – with hydration breaks effectively splitting matches into four quarters and allowing broadcasters to cut to adverts, and the 27-minute half-time interval at the World Cup final well in excess of the maximum 15 minutes permitted.

There are also no plans for Uefa to adopt other changes introduced for the World Cup – such as the interpretation of ‘mistaken identity’ VAR protocol which led to Switzerland’s Breel Embolo being sent off in the quarter-final against Argentina, or the dismissal of players who cover their mouths when talking to an opponent.

Ceferin has also been publicly critical of Fifa’s ticket pricing strategy for the tournament, with Uefa having revealed that 40 per cent of tickets for Euro 2028 will be in the most affordable categories and priced at around £30 or £60, and having also pledged not to adopt dynamic pricing.

Ceferin did attend the opening match of the tournament in Mexico City on 11 June.

title: Lamine Yamal celebrates World Cup win with little brother who charmed fans
description: The three-year-old brother of Yamal has been hailed one of Spain's World Cup breakout stars by fans
fullText:
Spanish footballer Lamine Yamal may have lifted the World Cup but it is his little brother Kenye who appears to have stolen the spotlight.

The three-year-old quickly become an internet sensation after footage of his exuberant celebrations following Spain's 1-0 victory over Argentina spread across social media on Sunday.

While the footballer, just 19 himself, continued his rise by helping Spain achieve football's biggest prize, attention swiftly turned to his younger brother's joyful reactions from the sidelines.

From sticking out his tongue on the big screen to wildly cheering on his older brother, Kenye has been hailed by fans as one of the unexpected breakout stars of the 2026 tournament.

Spain triumphed in a battle of endurance, beating Argentina with a goal in the 106th minute at the New York New Jersey Stadium.

The team's second World Cup win has led to Lamine Yamal becoming the youngest player ever to win both the European Championship and the World Cup.

His younger brother Kenye has become a regular fixture both on and off the pitch - often featuring in Lamine Yamal's TikToks and charming fans from around the world.

The three-year-old, who appears keen to follow in his big brother's footsteps, is the son of Lamine Yamal's mother Sheila Ebana.

Speaking about Kenye, Lamine Yamal previously told outlets: "I'm moved when I see my little brother this happy, as well as seeing my mum and friends living the life they always dreamed of.

"My little brother means everything to me. I am in love with him; it feels like he is my son."

In what could be seen as a symbolic passing of the torch, Lamine Yamal went head to head with Argentinan football legend Lionel Messi for the first time during the World Cup final.

The two had crossed paths years earlier in 2007 when Messi, then 20, was seen cradling and bathing a tiny baby boy - Lamine Yamal - after his mother won a competition to meet a Barcelona first-team player.

Days ahead of Sunday's final, Messi, now 39, described Lamine Yamal as "one of the best" footballers right now.

"He's a tremendous player - a global star. He's 19 years old and has his whole career ahead of him. I wish him the best, but we'll give it our all to ensure he doesn't become champion this time," he added.

As the full-time whistle blew, cementing Spain's victory, the pair could be seen embracing on the pitch.
