title: Experts warn of rise in sadistic online exploitation of vulnerable children
description:
Global increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse material
Cases of predators coercing children into producing sexual photos and videos of themselves have surged globally in the past year, a cruel phenomenon known as “sadistic online exploitation”, child safety experts and law enforcement officials warn.
In cases of sadistic online exploitation, perpetrators target vulnerable teens on social media platforms and online games. These predators then compel their victims into producing child sexual abuse material (CSAM), harming themselves and others, or, in extreme cases, attempting suicide.
In the US, call or text the Childhelp abuse hotline on 800-422-4453 or visit their website for more resources and to report child abuse or DM for help. For adult survivors of child abuse, help is available at ascasupport.
fullText:
Global increase in cases of predators coercing children to harm themselves or produce child sexual abuse material

Cases of predators coercing children into producing sexual photos and videos of themselves have surged globally in the past year, a cruel phenomenon known as “sadistic online exploitation”, child safety experts and law enforcement officials warn.

In cases of sadistic online exploitation, perpetrators target vulnerable teens on social media platforms and online games. These predators then compel their victims into producing child sexual abuse material (CSAM), harming themselves and others, or, in extreme cases, attempting suicide.

“We often see that the offenders will communicate with that child and, through that initial communication, ask them for nude or explicit imagery of themselves,” said Kathryn Rifenbark, a director at the National Center of Missing and Exploited Children (NCMEC). “Then they’re using that as a leverage to cause the child to harm themselves or harm other children or other people in their home or create more explicit imagery of them or other children in their home.”

Recent cases include the June sentencing of a 21-year-old Maryland man to 30 years in prison for sexually exploiting 10 female minors and cyberstalking.

The perpetrator “used the internet to create and share extreme content – such as gore, violence, and child sexual abuse material – to extort and blackmail vulnerable teenagers”, and the man sexually exploited his victims and encouraged them to engage in self-harm, according to a Department of Justice press release.

The NCMEC received more than 3,000 reports of sadistic online exploitation worldwide in 2025, a 125% increase from the previous year. But those cases represent only a small fraction of the total number of incidents, experts say, because the NCMEC is only notified if child sexual abuse material has been detected and reported.

Predators targeting children often belong to online networks, and are often teens or young adults themselves. To recruit their victims, the perpetrators engage with video games popular with children, such as Roblox and Minecraft. A spokesperson for Microsoft, which owns Minecraft, said: “Our goal is to make Minecraft one of the hardest places for bad actors to operate, and we have a variety of tools we use to help foster a safe online experience, such as parental controls, chat filtering, in-game reporting, and dedicated moderation teams.”

They may also search social media groups that focus on mental health issues such as eating disorders or suicide ideation because they are specifically looking for vulnerable children to target and groom, child safety experts interviewed said.

Perpetrators of sadistic online exploitation who coerce children into inflicting other forms of harm on themselves fall outside those reports.

The coerced abuses can include forcing children to cut themselves, eat their own vomit, drink urine, die by suicide, or harm other children and pets in their homes. Some groups also engage in mass casualty planning, doxing and cyber-harassment.

A common form of abuse is forcing victims to carve perpetrators’ social media usernames on their bodies, said Amanda Goharian, director of research at Thorn, a child safety non-profit.

“One of the key things in these groups is that the level of severity of the harms that are being committed are extreme, and they can rapidly develop into outcomes such as completed suicides,” said Goharian.

In February, the FBI issued a warning to parents and caregivers about the “sharp increase” in the activity of violent online networks, including the “764” group, targeting and exploiting children and other vulnerable individuals.

“The term nihilistic violent extremism (NVE) refers to what is motivating most of these subjects to commit criminal acts. They want to sow chaos and facilitate the destruction of society,” said the letter. “These subjects may be engaging in criminal activity for sexual gratification, social status, a sense of belonging, or for a mix of other reasons.”

The predators target vulnerable children who have disclosed personal information online about the struggles they face, such as mental health challenges, eating disorders, self-harm or suicidal ideation, via gaming or social media platforms. These children are looking for connections and support online, which can render them more susceptible to manipulation, said the experts interviewed.

Predators typically invite victims to move the conversation to a messaging app such as Telegram, which, according to Rifenbark, “doesn’t report very much” to the NCMEC, meaning the organization has limited visibility into potential child sexual exploitation.

It’s on these messaging platforms where the sadistic online exploitation takes place. Telegram did not respond to a request for comment.

Many of the perpetrators have troubled lives or are socially isolated in real life, and are looking for notoriety online as a form of empowerment and attention from others, experts interviewed said.

“These groups are focused on harming children and targeting children for the purposes of clout and like gaining hierarchy and power both within their groups and over the victims,” said Maria Thomas, investigator at Rise Information Security Foundation, a non-profit focused on cybersecurity. “Teens join them because it’s seen as a subversive and edgy thing to do.”

The perpetrators gain status within their groups from posting evidence of their abuse, images such as dossiers of photos and screenshots of messages with their victims, which are known as “lore books”. Typically, these collections are uploaded on to a Telegram channel labeled as “Victim Name Lore Book”, experts said.

“Harm is perpetrated as a way to essentially gain clout within the network, and to be elevated,” said Goharian.

Organized nihilistic violent extremism and sadistic online exploitation were first documented in the 2010s, when a youth cybergang called the Com, which focused on hacking and identity theft before some subsets shifted into child exploitation. Splinter networks, such as CVLT, an online group that espoused neo-Nazism, nihilism and pedophilia as its core principles, were formed. The group 764 was formed in 2021 by a teenager in Texas who had learned grooming and exploitation methods as a member of CVLT.

Online exploitation can escalate into dramatic real-world violence, experts warn. In February, an 18-year-old man from Pennsylvania was arrested and charged with grooming a 13-year-old girl he met on social gaming platform Discord, allegedly convincing her to send him sexually explicit material of herself and images of self-harm. The man travelled across the country to engage in sexual activity with the girl in a motel, according to the US justice department. “Discord has a longstanding policy on Teen and Child Safety that prohibits sexual exploitation and predatory behavior, including content or communities that facilitate grooming,” said a Discord spokesperson.

“When law enforcement arrived at the motel room, they encountered [the man] and the victim and found condoms, a knife, lubricant, razor blades, bloody tissues,” stated a Department of Justice press release about the case. “The victim told law enforcement that [the man] and she had engaged in sexual conduct and that he had used a knife to repeatedly cut her.”

In May, a 16-year-old boy from South Carolina was arrested for engaging in exploitation tied to 764 after receiving a tip from Discord regarding possible school violence.

“Offenders have a very, very high level of control over the children. They are using that connection that they’ve built with that child to really try to control that child to do some pretty harmful things to themselves,” said Rifenbark.

Rifenbark said the NCMEC had received reports from parents who discovered what was happening to their child and took away the child’s phone, only for the child to find another device in the home, such as an old device the family was no longer using, and use it to re-engage with the offenders online, probably due to the high levels of control and grooming they have been subjected to.

“A lot of times when victims are coming and reporting to us, they’re very scared, they’re scared of retaliation from offenders, they’re worried that their personal information might get leaked, because that’s something that the offenders are doing as part of their victimization,” Rifenbark said.

According to Goharian, the networks are highly persistent, with individual perpetrators often operating hundreds of accounts. If one account is reported for violating a platform’s terms of service and removed, they can quickly switch to “shadow” accounts that are already established and immediately resume their activity.

According to a state prosecutor, speaking on the condition of anonymity out of concern for professional reprisals, some juveniles who commit sexual abuse do not outgrow the behavior but often go on to offend as adults. Better interventions are needed for young offenders before their behavior escalates, they said.

“We just do a really bad job sometimes of recognizing dangerous kids,” said the prosecutor. “I don’t think these kids are going to grow out of it. I think they’re growing into it.”

In the US, call or text the Childhelp abuse hotline on 800-422-4453 or visit their website for more resources and to report child abuse or DM for help. For adult survivors of child abuse, help is available at ascasupport.org. In the UK, the NSPCC offers support to children on 0800 1111, and adults concerned about a child on 0808 800 5000. The National Association for People Abused in Childhood (Napac) offers support for adult survivors on 0808 801 0331. In Australia, children, young adults, parents and teachers can contact the Kids Helpline on 1800 55 1800, or Bravehearts on 1800 272 831, and adult survivors can contact Blue Knot Foundation on 1300 657 380. Other sources of help can be found at Child Helplines International

title: Villa sign Wolves midfielder Gomes in £38m deal
description: Aston Villa move for Brazil midfielder Joao Gomes after Amadou Onana suffered a serious knee injury at the World Cup.
fullText:
Aston Villa have completed the £38m signing of midfielder Joao Gomes from Wolves.

The Brazil international joins for an initial £34m with £4m in add-ons.

Villa completed a club record deal worth more than £50m for Freiburg midfielder Johan Manzambi on Friday, beating Newcastle to the Switzerland international.

Atletico Madrid had also been keen on Gomes, but the 25-year-old left Wolves' training camp in Portugal on Thursday to have a medical and finalise his move to Villa Park.

Villa midfielder Amadou Onana is out until next year after suffering a serious knee injury with Belgium at the World Cup.

Villa have also sold Youri Tielemans to Manchester United for £35m, while fellow midfielder Morgan Rogers is set to leave after a £117m deal was agreed with Chelsea.

Gomes made 41 appearances for Wolves last season and was named Player of the Year as they finished bottom of the Premier League.

He played 130 times for the club, scoring seven goals, after joining from Flamengo in 2023.

Wolves technical director Matt Jackson said: "Joao deserves everything he gets from the game. He's a model professional, a lovely young man and a great family man.

"He's the type of professional coaches love and players love, so he will go on to be the outstanding player that we know he is.

"We're obviously sorry to see him go, but we have to take responsible decisions for the football club, and then we build to be stronger collectively.

"It's a very natural part of football - the best run football clubs who don't have unlimited resources have to work this way.

"The model is something that we have to adopt, and our fans have always been hugely understanding of that."

Villa needed to move after Onana suffered an anterior cruciate ligament injury on duty at the World Cup with Belgium, which threw a major spanner in Villa's transfer plans this summer.

With Tielemans being sold, Villa had a gaping hole in their midfield, even if Boubacar Kamara is due to return from his own serious knee injury, which he suffered in January.

Gomes arrives with the experience and Premier League know-how to hit the ground running under manager Unai Emery.

Emery wants ready-made players equipped to understand his methods quickly and Gomes should fit the bill well.

A combative player, he made the third most tackles in the Premier League last season with 107, behind Everton's James Garner and Joao Palhinha, who was on loan at Tottenham from Bayern Munich and whom Villa also hold an interest in this summer.

Gomes' ball recoveries were the fourth highest in the division - he won possession 194 times.

With 116 Premier League appearances in his three and a half years at Molineux, there is plenty of experience as Villa move into a new era following last season's Europa League win.

Gomes arrives at a fair price with Villa needing to walk the financial tightrope to ensure they comply with Premier League and Uefa financial regulations.

Joao Gomes joins Villa with a statistical profile that shows exactly why the club have targeted him. Last season he was one of the Premier League's most active ball winners. He ranked third for tackles and second for both duels contested and duels won.

He also finished eighth for interceptions, which highlights how often he broke up play and how consistently he operated in the heart of Wolves' midfield.

The Brazilian's intensity without the ball was just as impressive. Gomes made more than 1,800 attempts to close down opponents, which placed him among the league's busiest pressers.

Villa's new midfielder was also trusted to take the ball in tight areas for now‑relegated Wolves, ranking sixth in the league for passes received under pressure.

One underrated element is his reliability. The best ability is availability - and he featured in 35 of Wolves' 38 league games.

His accuracy in those moments, almost 84%, is not elite but respectable. It is secure enough in possession for a high intensity midfielder.

On the ball, Gomes tends to carry play forward rather than rely on long or ambitious passing.

He ranked 14th for progressive carries and, although his forward passing numbers were lower, he did lead all Premier League midfielders for line-breaking passes played in the final third.

The only clear flaw in his profile is discipline. He conceded 69 fouls - the most in the league. He is combative, sometimes over zealous, but in bringing him to Villa Park, Unai Emery will believe that edge and ability to win the ball back will strengthen his side's midfield.

title: Best goal, best player, best fans - BBC reporters' World Cup awards
description: BBC Sport reporters pick their best moments of the tournament - and you can vote for yours.
fullText:
After delivering unforgettable drama and surprises across 104 matches over almost six weeks, the World Cup is over.

History has been scripted, records broken and heroes old and new made their mark at football's most coveted tournament.

Read our reporters' reflections from the US, Canada and Mexico and then choose from our chief football writer Phil McNulty's selections for best goal and best save.

Spain captain Rodri won the official World Cup Golden Ball trophy as the tournament's best player.

Messi won the Silver Ball as the second-best player, with Mbappe named Bronze Ball winner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Surprise, surprise. Lionel Messi. Not simply a swansong for a 39-year-old closing down his career, but the enduring inspiration of a nation and a football team. Look at how he controlled the game and pulled the strings when Argentina chased England down in a dramatic semi-final.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

I was in a Kylian Mbappe news conference early in the World Cup and I remember thinking that I'd never seen him looking more relaxed, more confident and more determined to make this his tournament. That demeanour off the pitch was reflected in some brilliant performances on the pitch - great goals, leadership, flair, composure under pressure and more work off the ball than I've ever seen from him.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The player of the tournament has been Jude Bellingham. The Real Madrid midfielder dragged England through by sheer force of will in some games and took his game to another level during the tournament.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Lionel Messi's leadership, football intelligence and ability to decide the biggest matches has proven vital for Argentina. He was the driving force behind their run to the final and that makes him the competition's standout player.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

With eight goals and four assists in eight games at this World Cup, it has to be Lionel Messi. England did their best to keep him quiet in the semi-final and succeeded in the first half, but Messi often finds a way and his two assists proved decisive.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Michael Olise. Marked out of the semi-final against Spain but the former Crystal Palace forward, who started his journey aged six at Hayes & Yeading United, has left his mark on this tournament with his seven assists. At just 24, Olise has another World Cup or two up his sleeve. I'm still playing replays in my mind of that spectacular scissor-kick against Sweden in the last 32. The whole press box erupted thinking it was going in, only for the ball to crash against a post.

Rodri. He has proved to be irreplaceable for his country as he is for Manchester City. Exceptional and so effective at the heart of the best team in the tournament

Easy choice but it has to be Lionel Messi. Doing this at the age of 39 is almost defying the law of physics. He is cementing his place as the greatest.

Spain's 19-year-old centre-back Pau Cubarsi received the Fifa award for the tournament's best young player after playing every minute of their World Cup campaign.

He was a key figure in the Spain defence that conceded only one goal all tournament.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

I am going for Spain's 19-year-old central defender Pau Cubarsi. The Barcelona youngster was magnificent against France in the semi-final and is a star of the future.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Lille fans, regular viewers of Ligue 1 and recruitment teams from the top clubs knew all about his potential but Morocco's Ayyoub Bouaddi burst on to the world stage in style with an exceptional performance in their opening match against Brazil. At the New York New Jersey Stadium that day, I found myself not watching the ball but watching his incredibly intelligent movement around the pitch, which reminded me of a cross between Patrick Vieira and Luka Modric.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

It sounds strange to call Erling Haaland a breakout star but it is hard to explain the impact he has had here in the US. He has become a household name and even more of a global superstar.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cape Verde's 40-year-old goalkeeper Vozinha. He began his unforgettable World Cup with seven sensational saves in a 0–0 draw against Spain in their debut World Cup match and ended it with eight more remarkable saves as Cape Verde pushed Lionel Messi and Argentina to the brink of elimination. Vozinha's heroics sparked a global following, with his Instagram audience skyrocketing from about 50,000 before the tournament to more than 29 million.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

It seemed like the USA would go far at this tournament, particularly with the wave of support behind them, but it wasn't to be as they exited at the last-16 stage. Despite that, many of their players did their own reputations no harm and one of those was full-back Alex Freeman, with his performances leading to USA boss Mauricio Pochettino saying he could be one of the best in the world in his position.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Step forward Switzerland's Johan Manzambi. Some 20-year-olds might be overwhelmed playing on the biggest stage of all. Not Manzambi. This huge talent plays with a smile on his face and is a natural entertainer who departed the tournament with three goals in four games.

Johan Manzambi. I highlighted him before the World Cup and, although he missed the end for Switzerland through injury, the fact he is moving to the Premier League with Aston Villa for a fee in the region of £50m underlines his quality.

The 17-year-old Mexico midfielder Gilberto Mora, who ended up in their starting line-up. Had the air of Luka Modric and seems destined for the top of the game.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The Azteca for Mexico v England. Might just be the best atmosphere I have ever experienced in seven World Cups. It was an assault on the senses and the singing of the Mexican national anthem was a spine-tingling experience. A simply unforgettable night.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Speaking to the Paraguay fans outside the Boston stadium, moments after one of the most dramatic penalty shootouts I have ever seen, as the South American side stunned Germany. One father and son, draped in Paraguay flags, were in tears as they told me that this was the best day of their lives, and it summed up what special moments at a World Cup can mean to people. I will never forget the emotion of those fans streaming out of the ground in disbelief.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Lionel Messi scoring a hat-trick in his first game at this World Cup showed that he was not done and was going to try to push Argentina to another tournament win.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Sidny Cabral's stunning 103rd-minute equaliser against Argentina. His strike from the edge of the penalty area that flew beyond Emiliano Martinez sent the stadium into disbelief and brought Cape Verde level at 2-2 in extra time. Although Argentina eventually edged the tie 3-2, Cabral's wonder goal became an instant classic and will always be talked about.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

For me it was Argentina being pushed by Cape Verde all the way before the debutants were knocked out. They showed the sort of guts and desire we want to see from these teams that gained the opportunity to play at a World Cup because of the expanded format.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

On my travels around the US, where I have covered 13 games in six different cities, I have spoken to dozens of fans. I was offered a beer and sausages cooked from the back of a pick-up truck before France-Paraguay in Philadelphia, and been stopped for selfies. "You're from the BBC? I have to have a selfie with the guy from the BBC." I have lost count of the number of times I have stopped a fan and introduced myself, only to be asked: "Is it coming home?"

For the moment I actually witnessed, it would have to be Sidny Cabral and his sensational equaliser against Argentina as Cape Verde, the smallest nation to ever reach the knockout stages, stunned the defending champions for a second time to make it 2-2 in Miami.

Cabral's astonishing goal against Argentina. It was as funny as it was breathtaking.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Brazil. Slow. Uninspired. Un-Brazilian. Trudged through the World Cup under Carlo Ancelotti and every weakness was exposed when they deservedly went out to Norway in the last 16.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Senegal came to the tournament with the most talented squad in their history but various problems off the pitch arguably meant the players were not given the right environment to thrive. When they were 2-0 up in the last-32 match against Belgium, it looked as though they would salvage something from this World Cup, but tactical errors and poor game management cost them. It was a massive wasted opportunity for a gifted generation of Senegal players.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Scotland missing out on third-placed qualification was a disappointment and having to wait a couple of days before it was confirmed must have been painful.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cristiano Ronaldo and Portugal. As one of the pre-tournament favourites, they fell short after a 1–0 last-16 defeat by Spain. Ronaldo managed three goals in five matches and, despite high expectations, Portugal never reached the level many expected, ending another World Cup campaign in frustrating fashion.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

The USA. They started this tournament so well and created a real wave of optimism and hope among the home fans, but all that was undone by the controversy surrounding Folarin Balogun and then their limp exit at the hands of Belgium.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

The final being held at the MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. Yes, it is huge but it is not fan-friendly and it is an expensive pain to get to and get away from. Philadelphia would have been my pick. A stadium on the outskirts of the city in a sports complex. The MetLife is a giant bowl in the middle of nowhere.

France in their semi-final defeat by Spain. I tipped Spain to beat France but still did not expect such a flat performance and their star-studded frontline to flounder as badly as they did.

I really thought Brazil under Ancelotti would go further. And clearly so did they.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Mexico. The sheer passion and love for their team and football was incredible. Also such friendly, welcoming fans and people. Incredibly warm and gracious in defeat.

We were in a restaurant in Mexico City the night before they played England, and an MC went around each table asking which country people were from. When we told them we were from England, it was announced to the rest of the diners, who burst into a spontaneous outbreak of applause.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

A word for the Curacao fans I was with inside the Philadelphia Stadium - because who knew that supporters from a nation of just 150,000 could make that much noise? Was there anyone left on the island? But the Brazil fans win it for me because they do not just take over stadiums; they take over cities; Philadelphia felt like Rio or Sao Paulo for 48 hours when they were in town.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The Colombia fans took over Arrowhead Stadium in Kansas City when they played Ghana, and made it feel like a party.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's fans were brilliant, with their legendary Viking row taking over the World Cup. The chant became a global sensation and created an unforgettable atmosphere in every stadium they entered. The most iconic moments came after matches when the players joined the fans to lead the Viking row from the pitch, uniting thousands of supporters in celebration.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

I was based mainly in Los Angeles for the World Cup. There's a large Mexican population there and from what I experienced, they were undoubtedly the best supporters. They were loud - very loud - for every Mexico game and when they won fireworks could be heard around LA while their distinct green jerseys were seen everywhere.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's Viking row was fun to watch, with fans wearing red shirts sitting in lines and rowing in rhythm. The Norway supporters also provided an amusing moment during the quarter-final with England. They sang "You're going home, you're going home, England's going home" as they led 1-0 in Miami. Moments later Jude Bellingham equalised.

The Mexicans. I was fortunate to experience two matches in the Azteca. That is where the heart of this World Cup was and the support for their team was a joy to behold.

Argentina. On two occasions in Miami and Kansas City, I have been in stadiums where their support has been in excess of 60,000 fans. The sheer scale of numbers is matched by their wild passion. They sing constantly and have generated the best atmospheres I have encountered in the US.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The deadlock was broken by Lionel Messi for Argentina against Cape Verde with a triumph of technique as he showed exquisite touch to take a difficult pass on the run before, with little room to work with, he guided it into the top corner with his second touch.

In the same game, Sidny Cabral's extra-time equaliser was a wonderful moment. From the angle just inside the area, his right-foot curling drive into the top corner gave Emiliano Martinez no chance.

In the group stages, Kerim Alajbegovic's goal for Bosnia-Herzegovina against Qatar was a superb solo effort - a weaving run before a rising right-foot shot of high class.

What looked like a casual swing of Erling Haaland's left boot was in fact a perfectly placed finish with astonishing power to crown a match-winning performance for Norway against Brazil in the last 16.

A tight quarter-final between Argentina and Switzerland was broken open by a moment of genius from Julian Alvarez. The vastly under-rated attacker provided with a swerving right-footed drive from long range into the top corner in extra time.

Antonio Nusa's opener for Norway against Ivory Coast was perfection. A little shimmy in the area to feint away from a defender, then a precise curling finish into the top corner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Johny Placide made a superb double save for Haiti against Morocco. It came first with his legs then he showed swift reactions to save one-handed to his left.

At the Azteca Stadium, Jordan Pickford had an outstanding game in England's memorable win over Mexico. His early save, diving low to his left to keep out Raul Jimenez's header, was a crucial moment of brilliance.

Orjan Nyland produced a remarkable stop for Norway against Brazil from his own defender Kristoffer Ajer, who inadvertently directed the ball goalwards. Nyland scrambled backwards showing remarkably anticipation and agility to leap and turn the ball against the post.

Aymen Dahmen showed stunning reactions for Tunisia against Japan from Takehiro Tomiyasu's close-range effort, somehow keeping the ball out as it did everything but cross the line.

There was a brilliant diving save by Patrick Beach for Australia in their group game with Turkey, diving away to his right to touch Abdulkerim Bardakci's rising 30-yard shot on to the post.

Iran's Alireza Beiranvand produced a remarkable save from Maxim de Cuyper of Belgium. Beiranvand could not reach Kevin de Bruyne's shot but somehow recovered his position and reacted to keep out a shot from inside the six-yard box.
